abstract: Electrochemical synthesis offers a sustainable pathway for producing high-value organonitrogen chemicals using renewable energy and environmental pollutants. Here, we investigate the use of BIF-29(Cu), a molecular boron-imidazolate cage featuring isolated copper sites, as an electrocatalyst for the synthesis of urea through the co-reduction of $CO_2$ and $NO_3^-$. Despite its single-atom catalytic centers, BIF-29(Cu) achieves an exceptional urea Faradaic efficiency of 68.5% and a partial current density of 424 µA cm⁻², placing it among the most selective systems reported to date for this transformation. The reaction mechanism was elucidated using a combination of in situ surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (SEIRAS), in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Our findings reveal that the C-N bond formation proceeds via a cascade mechanism: $NO_3^-$ and $CO_2$ are independently reduced at distinct copper sites to generate hydroxylamine ($NH_2OH$) and adsorbed carbon monoxide (*CO), respectively. The subsequent condensation of these intermediates at a single copper site facilitates the final urea production. This study demonstrates the potential of supramolecular metal-organic cages with well-defined, discrete active sites to mediate complex, multi-step coupling reactions with high efficiency and structural stability.
